title: anthemis cotula
intro: Anthemis cotula, also known as dog fennel or stinking chamomile, is a perennial herb belonging to the sunflower family. The plant has been used for centuries in traditional medicine due to its medicinal properties, which include its antiseptic, anti-inflammatory, and antispasmodic properties. The health benefits of anthemis cotula include its ability to soothe digestive issues, reduce anxiety and stress, and alleviate menstrual cramps and other female reproductive issues. Anthemis cotula can be prepared as a herbal tea, infusion, or tincture, and is often used in aromatherapy to promote relaxation and calmness.
intro_study: According to the provided study, there is no mention of the medicinal purposes of Anthemis cotula, as the study focuses on the relationship between slavery and plant knowledge on Isle de France (Mauritius) in the 18th century.
benefits_desc: Anthemis cotula is used to provide a range of health benefits, from soothing digestive issues and alleviating menstrual cramps to reducing inflammation and promoting a good night's sleep. This versatile plant has also been known to help alleviate symptoms of anxiety and depression, improve respiratory function, and even support the immune system. Its medicinal uses extend to treating skin conditions such as eczema and acne, as well as reducing fever and relieving headaches. By incorporating anthemis cotula into one's wellness routine, individuals can experience a multitude of health benefits and improve their overall quality of life.

actions_desc: Anthemis cotula has been reported to exhibit various therapeutic actions, including anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and nervine properties. These actions affect the musculoskeletal system, cardiovascular system, and central nervous system, reducing inflammation and spasms, while also exerting a calming effect on the nervous system. Additionally, Anthemis cotula displays anthelmintic, antiseptic, and antiemetic properties, which impact the gastrointestinal system, immune system, and sensory system, respectively. Furthermore, it also exhibits expectorant, diuretic, and vulnerary actions, influencing the respiratory system, urinary system, and integumentary system.

constituents_desc: Anthemis cotula has been found to contain an array of bioactive constituents, including flavonoids, such as apigenin and luteolin, which exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The plant also contains tannins, which have been shown to possess antimicrobial and astringent properties, while its coumarins, including scopoletin and umbelliferone, have been found to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. Additionally, Anthemis cotula contains sesquiterpenes, such as chamissonite and chamissonol, which have been reported to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, as well as phenolic acids, including ferulic and sinapic acids, which have been found to have antimicrobial and antioxidant properties. These bioactive constituents contribute to the plant's potential health benefits.

parts_desc: The most commonly used parts of Anthemis cotula are its leaves, roots, and flowers, which have been utilized in various ways for health purposes. The leaves of the Anthemis cotula plant are used in traditional medicine to treat conditions such as fever, headaches, and skin issues, and are also used as an ingredient in teas and infusions to aid digestion and reduce stress. The roots of the plant are used to treat respiratory issues like bronchitis and asthma, and are also employed in the treatment of urinary tract problems and menstrual cramps. Additionally, the flowers of the Anthemis cotula plant are used to make herbal infusions that help to soothe anxiety and insomnia, and are also used in skincare products to reduce inflammation and promote wound healing.

preparations_desc: Anthemis cotula most common herbal preparations are typically used to create infusions, tinctures, and essential oils for various health purposes. The infusions, also known as herbal teas, are commonly used to treat digestive issues such as bloating and indigestion, while also providing relief from stress and anxiety, improving sleep quality, and aiding in the treatment of menstrual cramps and PMS symptoms. The tinctures are often used to address skin issues like acne and eczema, as well as to provide relief from colds, flu, and other respiratory infections. Additionally, the essential oils derived from Anthemis cotula are used in aromatherapy to reduce inflammation, promote relaxation, and ease symptoms of migraines and headaches.

side_effects_desc: Anthemis cotula side effects are primarily associated with its use in herbal remedies and are more likely to be experienced by individuals consuming large quantities or using it for extended periods. Common side effects include dizziness, headaches, stomach pain, nausea, and vomiting, as well as allergic reactions such as skin irritation, itching, and hives. Additionally, Anthemis cotula may cause cardiovascular issues, such as rapid heartbeat and blood pressure fluctuations, and some users have reported neurological symptoms like tremors, seizures, and confusion. To minimize potential side effects, it is recommended to use Anthemis cotula in moderation, avoid consuming it if you have pre-existing health conditions, and be cautious of interactions with other medications, especially those affecting the heart and nervous system.

side_effects_list.name: allergic contact dermatitis
side_effects_list.desc: an allergic reaction that causes skin irritation, redness, and itching upon contact with the plant's sap

side_effects_list.desc: nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea caused by ingesting the plant's leaves or flowers

side_effects_list.desc: coughing, sneezing, and difficulty breathing due to inhaling the plant's pollen or volatile oils

side_effects_list.desc: redness, itching, and tearing of the eyes caused by exposure to the plant's sap or pollen

side_effects_list.name: skin irritation
side_effects_list.desc: redness, itching, and inflammation of the skin caused by contact with the plant's sap or volatile oils

side_effects_list.name: photodermatitis
side_effects_list.desc: skin irritation and blisters caused by exposure to sunlight after contact with the plant's sap

side_effects_list.name: anaphylaxis
side_effects_list.desc: a severe, life-threatening allergic reaction that can cause symptoms such as difficulty breathing, rapid heartbeat, and a drop in blood pressure
